edges.title: Warfarin + Aspirin: High
edges.description: Concurrent use significantly increases bleeding risk. Both inhibit platelet function through different mechanisms. Monitor for signs of bleeding (bruising, hematoma, blood in stool/urine). Consider ga...

edges.title: Warfarin + Ibuprofen: High
edges.description: NSAIDs increase bleeding risk 2-3 fold when combined with warfarin. Ibuprofen inhibits platelet COX-1, impairing aggregation. Monitor INR closely; may need warfarin dose reduction.

edges.title: Warfarin + Naproxen: High
edges.description: Naproxen prolongs bleeding time and may displace warfarin from protein binding sites. Risk of major hemorrhage. Monitor INR every 3-7 days after NSAID initiation.

edges.title: Warfarin + Meloxicam: High
edges.description: COX-2 selective NSAIDs still increase bleeding risk with anticoagulants. Less GI toxicity than non-selective NSAIDs but bleeding risk remains. Monitor closely.

edges.title: Warfarin + Diclofenac: High
edges.description: Diclofenac increases warfarin effect via pharmacodynamic interaction. Risk of serious bleeding events. Use lowest effective NSAID dose for shortest duration.

edges.title: Apixaban + Aspirin: High
edges.description: Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) plus antiplatelet increases major bleeding risk 1.5-2 fold. Often used together post-ACS but requires careful risk-benefit assessment.

edges.title: Apixaban + Ibuprofen: High
edges.description: NSAID use with DOAC increases bleeding risk without providing additional thrombotic protection. Avoid concurrent use when possible; use acetaminophen for pain.

edges.title: Apixaban + Naproxen: High
edges.description: Combined antiplatelet effect significantly increases hemorrhage risk. If NSAID necessary, use lowest dose for shortest time and counsel patient on bleeding signs.

edges.title: Rivaroxaban + Aspirin: High
edges.description: Aspirin plus rivaroxaban acceptable only when clear indication exists (e.g., post-PCI). Increases bleeding without reducing stroke risk in atrial fibrillation alone.

edges.title: Rivaroxaban + Ibuprofen: High
edges.description: NSAID use contraindicated with rivaroxaban unless benefits clearly outweigh bleeding risks. Consider alternatives like acetaminophen or topical NSAIDs.

edges.title: Rivaroxaban + Naproxen: High
edges.description: Naproxen has long half-life (12-17 hours), providing prolonged antiplatelet effect. This increases bleeding risk when combined with DOAC. Avoid combination.

edges.title: Clopidogrel + Aspirin: Medium
edges.description: Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) standard post-stent but increases bleeding 2-3 fold. Duration depends on stent type and bleeding risk. Monitor closely.

edges.title: Apixaban + Clopidogrel: High
edges.description: Triple therapy (DOAC + dual antiplatelet) reserved for specific situations (recent PCI + atrial fib). Very high bleeding risk. Shorten DAPT duration when possible.

edges.title: Rivaroxaban + Clopidogrel: High
edges.description: Similar to apixaban + clopidogrel. Major bleeding rate 2-3% annually. Use only when benefits outweigh risks. Consider PPI for gastroprotection.

edges.title: Warfarin + Azithromycin: Medium
edges.description: Macrolides may potentiate warfarin via unknown mechanism. Monitor INR within 3-5 days of starting azithromycin. Expect potential 20-30% INR increase. Consider prophylactic warfarin dose reduction of 1...

edges.title: Warfarin + Ciprofloxacin: High
edges.description: Fluoroquinolones significantly increase warfarin levels via CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 inhibition. INR may increase 50-100%. Reduce warfarin dose 25-50% empirically. Check INR every 2-3 days during therapy.

edges.title: Warfarin + Levofloxacin: High
edges.description: Similar to ciprofloxacin. Levofloxacin inhibits warfarin metabolism, increasing bleeding risk. Reduce warfarin dose proactively. Multiple case reports of serious bleeding events.

edges.title: Warfarin + Metronidazole: High
edges.description: Potent CYP2C9 inhibition causes 30-50% increase in warfarin levels. One of the strongest warfarin interactions. Reduce dose 30-40% when starting metronidazole. Check INR frequently (every 2-3 days).

edges.title: Warfarin + Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole: High
edges.description: Stereoselective inhibition of S-warfarin metabolism (more potent enantiomer). Can cause dramatic INR increases (>10 reported). High bleeding risk. Consider alternative antibiotic if possible.

edges.title: Warfarin + Clindamycin: Low
edges.description: Limited interaction data. Theoretically may alter gut flora affecting vitamin K production. Monitor INR but significant changes uncommon. Generally safe combination.

edges.title: Warfarin + Amoxicillin: Low
edges.description: Beta-lactams have minimal direct interaction with warfarin. May reduce vitamin K producing gut bacteria with prolonged use. Monitor INR if therapy >7-10 days.

edges.title: Warfarin + Doxycycline: Medium
edges.description: Tetracyclines may enhance warfarin effect through unclear mechanism. Monitor INR but interaction less pronounced than with fluoroquinolones or metronidazole.

edges.title: Warfarin + Cephalexin: Low
edges.description: First-generation cephalosporins have minimal warfarin interaction. Routine INR monitoring sufficient. Generally safe combination.

edges.title: Warfarin + Nitrofurantoin: Low
edges.description: Minimal interaction reported. Safe for UTI treatment in patients on warfarin. Standard INR monitoring adequate.

edges.title: Sertraline + Tramadol: High
edges.description: Both increase serotonin. Risk of serotonin syndrome (SS): agitation, confusion, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, hyperreflexia, myoclonus. SS can be fatal. Avoid combination; use non-serotoner...

edges.title: Escitalopram + Tramadol: High
edges.description: Escitalopram is potent SSRI; tramadol inhibits serotonin/norepinephrine reuptake. High SS risk especially with tramadol doses >200mg/day. Consider alternatives: acetaminophen, NSAIDs, or opioids witho...

edges.title: Fluoxetine + Tramadol: High
edges.description: Fluoxetine has long half-life (4-6 days) and active metabolite (7-9 days). Combined with tramadol, creates prolonged SS risk. Also increases seizure risk via lowered seizure threshold. Strongly avoid ...

edges.title: Citalopram + Tramadol: High
edges.description: Similar mechanism to other SSRIs. SS onset can be within hours or take several days. Early symptoms: restlessness, diarrhea, tremor. Severe: rigidity, seizures, autonomic instability. Educate patients...

edges.title: Paroxetine + Tramadol: High
edges.description: Paroxetine is potent CYP2D6 inhibitor, reducing tramadol metabolism to active metabolite (M1). Creates dual risk: SS from serotonin excess, plus reduced analgesia. Avoid combination.

edges.title: Duloxetine + Tramadol: High
edges.description: SNRI (affects both serotonin and norepinephrine) plus tramadol creates very high SS risk. Duloxetine also used for pain, making tramadol potentially unnecessary. Consider if adequate pain control with...

edges.title: Venlafaxine + Tramadol: High
edges.description: Venlafaxine at doses >150mg/day has significant serotonergic effect. Combined with tramadol, substantially increases SS risk. Monitor closely if combination unavoidable; use lowest tramadol dose.

edges.title: Sertraline + Trazodone: Medium
edges.description: Trazodone used for insomnia in depression patients on SSRIs. Low-dose trazodone (25-100mg) generally safe but monitor for SS symptoms. Higher trazodone doses increase risk.

edges.title: Fluoxetine + Bupropion: Medium
edges.description: Not traditional serotonin syndrome but fluoxetine inhibits bupropion metabolism via CYP2D6, increasing seizure risk. Monitor for agitation, insomnia. Generally safe combination but requires monitoring...

edges.title: Alprazolam + Tramadol: High
edges.description: FDA BLACK BOX WARNING: Benzodiazepine-opioid combination increases risk of profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, death. Over 30% of opioid overdose deaths involve benzodiazepines. Avoid com...

edges.title: Lorazepam + Tramadol: High
edges.description: Additive CNS and respiratory depression. Elderly especially vulnerable. If combination unavoidable: start low doses (e.g., lorazepam 0.5mg, tramadol 25mg), titrate slowly, educate patient/family on ov...

edges.title: Diazepam + Tramadol: High
edges.description: Diazepam has long half-life (20-100 hours) and active metabolites, creating prolonged interaction risk. Additive sedation and respiratory depression. Strongly avoid; consider non-benzodiazepine altern...

edges.title: Clonazepam + Tramadol: High
edges.description: Similar risks to other benzodiazepines. Clonazepam often used long-term for seizures/anxiety, making interaction more clinically relevant. Discuss tapering benzodiazepine before starting opioid, or us...

edges.title: Alprazolam + Gabapentin: Medium
edges.description: Both cause CNS depression but respiratory depression risk lower than with opioids. Still increases fall risk (especially elderly), sedation, cognitive impairment. Monitor closely; consider dose reduct...

edges.title: Lorazepam + Gabapentin: Medium
edges.description: Additive dizziness, drowsiness, confusion. Falls are major concern in elderly. Start gabapentin at low dose (100-300mg/day) if patient on benzodiazepine. Consider fall risk assessment.

edges.title: Diazepam + Gabapentin: Medium
edges.description: Enhanced sedation and ataxia. Elderly at high risk for falls and hip fractures. If combination needed, use lowest effective doses and implement fall precautions (remove trip hazards, use assistive dev...

edges.title: Alprazolam + Pregabalin: Medium
edges.description: Similar to gabapentin interaction. Pregabalin may have stronger sedative effect than gabapentin. Monitor for excessive drowsiness, impaired coordination. Avoid driving/operating machinery until effect...

edges.title: Lorazepam + Pregabalin: Medium
edges.description: Additive CNS effects. Pregabalin has abuse potential, as do benzodiazepines; combination may be sought by drug-seeking patients. Monitor for misuse signs. Consider non-addictive alternatives.

edges.title: Alprazolam + Zolpidem: Medium
edges.description: Both are CNS depressants for sleep. Combination provides no additional benefit and significantly increases next-day sedation, fall risk, complex sleep behaviors. Use one agent, not both.

edges.title: Lorazepam + Zolpidem: Medium
edges.description: Redundant therapy with increased adverse effects. If patient has both anxiety and insomnia, consider using single benzodiazepine at bedtime rather than adding zolpidem. Reduces polypharmacy.

edges.title: Diphenhydramine + Alprazolam: Medium
edges.description: First-generation antihistamine has strong anticholinergic and sedative effects. Combined with benzodiazepine, increases delirium risk in elderly (Beers Criteria - avoid this combination in ≥65 years o...

edges.title: Diphenhydramine + Lorazepam: Medium
edges.description: Additive anticholinergic effects: dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, confusion. In elderly, can precipitate delirium or worsen dementia. Prefer non-sedating antihistamine (loratadine, cetiriz...

edges.title: Diphenhydramine + Zolpidem: Medium
edges.description: Both cause sedation and increase fall risk. Next-day "hangover" effect common. Avoid using OTC sleep aids (diphenhydramine) if already on prescription sleep medication.

edges.title: Lisinopril + Spironolactone: Medium
edges.description: ACE inhibitor reduces aldosterone, causing K+ retention. Spironolactone is aldosterone antagonist, also retaining K+. Combined effect can cause dangerous hyperkalemia (K+ >5.5 mEq/L). Monitor K+ withi...

edges.title: Lisinopril + Potassium Chloride: Medium
edges.description: ACE inhibitor plus K+ supplement significantly increases hyperkalemia risk. Often patients started on K+ for diuretic-induced hypokalemia, then ACE inhibitor added later. Reassess need for K+ suppleme...

edges.title: Losartan + Spironolactone: Medium
edges.description: ARB plus aldosterone antagonist: both reduce K+ excretion. Particularly problematic in heart failure patients who may be on both for proven mortality benefit. Requires very close K+ monitoring (weekly...

edges.title: Losartan + Potassium Chloride: Medium
edges.description: Similar to lisinopril + K+ interaction. ARBs increase K+ retention. Reassess need for supplementation. If patient on HCTZ or furosemide causing hypokalemia, ARB may normalize K+ without need for suppl...

edges.title: Lisinopril + Ibuprofen: Medium
edges.description: Triple whammy for kidneys: ACE inhibitor + NSAID + (often) diuretic. NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow, ACE inhibitors dilate efferent arteriole - combined effect impairs GFR. Can precipitate acute kidne...

edges.title: Losartan + Naproxen: Medium
edges.description: ARB plus NSAID impairs kidney function, reducing K+ excretion. Especially risky in elderly, volume depleted, or pre-existing CKD. If NSAID necessary, use shortest duration, monitor renal function and ...

edges.title: Naproxen + Lisinopril: Medium
edges.description: Similar to ibuprofen. Naproxen has longer half-life (12-17 hours), providing more sustained prostaglandin inhibition and BP elevation. Monitor BP weekly for first month if combination unavoidable.

edges.title: Ibuprofen + Losartan: Medium
edges.description: NSAIDs reduce ARB antihypertensive efficacy. Mechanism: decreased renal prostaglandin synthesis impairs pressure natriuresis. Clinical significance: 10-15% of patients may lose BP control. Consider AR...

edges.title: Meloxicam + Lisinopril: Medium
edges.description: COX-2 selective NSAID still interferes with ACE inhibitor. Less GI toxicity than non-selective NSAIDs but similar BP effects. Not preferred over non-pharmacologic pain management or acetaminophen.

edges.title: Ibuprofen + Amlodipine: Low
edges.description: NSAIDs have minimal effect on calcium channel blocker efficacy. CCBs work via direct vasodilation, not prostaglandin-dependent. Generally safe combination; monitor BP but significant changes uncommon.

edges.title: Ibuprofen + Metoprolol: Low
edges.description: NSAIDs do not significantly affect beta blocker antihypertensive effect. However, NSAIDs may worsen heart failure (fluid retention), which beta blockers help treat. Monitor for edema, weight gain.

edges.title: Ibuprofen + Hydrochlorothiazide: Medium
edges.description: NSAIDs reduce diuretic natriuretic and antihypertensive effects. Mechanism: decreased renal prostaglandins impair response to diuretics. May cause fluid retention (2-3 kg weight gain). Monitor BP, wei...

edges.title: Naproxen + Hydrochlorothiazide: Medium
edges.description: Similar to ibuprofen + HCTZ. Naproxen may cause more Na+ and fluid retention due to longer half-life. Can precipitate heart failure exacerbation in susceptible patients. Monitor volume status closely.

edges.title: Ibuprofen + Furosemide: Medium
edges.description: NSAIDs blunt loop diuretic response. Problematic in heart failure patients where diuresis critical. May require furosemide dose increase (sometimes 2-3x) to overcome NSAID effect. Avoid combination in...

edges.title: Meloxicam + Furosemide: Medium
edges.description: COX-2 selective NSAID still impairs loop diuretic efficacy. Not exempt from interaction. If pain control needed in HF patient, consider alternatives: topical NSAIDs, acetaminophen, or opioids (cautiou...

edges.title: Omeprazole + Clopidogrel: Medium
edges.description: Omeprazole is potent CYP2C19 inhibitor. Clopidogrel is prodrug requiring CYP2C19 activation. Omeprazole reduces active metabolite formation by 40-50%, potentially decreasing antiplatelet effect. Alter...

edges.title: Esomeprazole + Clopidogrel: Medium
edges.description: S-isomer of omeprazole with similar CYP2C19 inhibition. Reduces clopidogrel effectiveness. FDA recommends avoiding this combination. If PPI necessary for GI protection, use pantoprazole or consider H2...

edges.title: Pantoprazole + Clopidogrel: Low
edges.description: Minimal CYP2C19 inhibition compared to omeprazole/esomeprazole. Preferred PPI for patients on clopidogrel. Some residual interaction possible but clinically insignificant in most patients. Generally c...

edges.title: Lansoprazole + Clopidogrel: Medium
edges.description: Moderate CYP2C19 inhibition. Less than omeprazole but more than pantoprazole. If patient already on lansoprazole + clopidogrel, consider switching to pantoprazole. Clinical significance debated but pr...

edges.title: Omeprazole + Aspirin: Low
edges.description: Aspirin is not a prodrug; PPI does not reduce antiplatelet effect. PPIs recommended for GI protection in patients on aspirin, especially if dual antiplatelet therapy or high bleeding risk. Safe combin...

edges.title: Metformin + Lisinopril: Medium
edges.description: ACE inhibitors can reduce renal function, especially in bilateral renal artery stenosis or volume depletion. Impaired metformin clearance increases lactic acidosis risk. Monitor renal function (SCr, e...

edges.title: Metformin + Losartan: Medium
edges.description: Similar to ACE inhibitor interaction. ARBs reduce GFR via efferent arteriole dilation. In diabetic nephropathy, ARB benefits usually outweigh risks, but requires close renal monitoring. Adjust metform...

edges.title: Metformin + Furosemide: Medium
edges.description: Loop diuretic can cause volume depletion and pre-renal azotemia, impairing metformin clearance. Lactic acidosis risk increases. Monitor: volume status, renal function, signs of lactic acidosis (nausea...

edges.title: Metformin + Hydrochlorothiazide: Low
edges.description: Thiazide diuretic usually does not significantly impair renal function at standard doses. Monitor renal function routinely but metformin dose adjustment rarely needed. Ensure adequate hydration.

edges.title: Metoprolol + Diltiazem: Medium
edges.description: Both slow AV conduction and reduce heart rate. Risk: severe bradycardia (<50 bpm), AV block, hypotension. Often used together for rate control in atrial fibrillation but requires close monitoring. Che...

edges.title: Metoprolol + Verapamil: Medium
edges.description: Similar to diltiazem interaction. Verapamil has stronger negative inotropic effect; higher risk of heart failure exacerbation. Monitor: HR, BP, signs of HF (edema, dyspnea, weight gain). Use with caut...

edges.title: Carvedilol + Diltiazem: Medium
edges.description: Carvedilol is non-selective beta blocker with alpha-blocking properties, causing more hypotension than metoprolol. Combined with diltiazem: significant bradycardia and hypotension risk. Common in HF p...

edges.title: Carvedilol + Verapamil: Medium
edges.description: High-risk combination. Both have negative inotropic effects. Can precipitate acute HF decompensation. Generally avoided. If both needed, consider: beta blocker + dihydropyridine CCB (amlodipine) inste...

edges.title: Atenolol + Diltiazem: Medium
edges.description: Beta-1 selective blocker plus non-dihydropyridine CCB. Lower risk than carvedilol combinations but still requires monitoring. Additive effects on HR and conduction. Monitor ECG for PR interval prolong...

edges.title: Atenolol + Verapamil: Medium
edges.description: Similar to atenolol + diltiazem. Verapamil more potent; slightly higher interaction risk. Combination sometimes used but requires close follow-up, especially in elderly or those with baseline bradycar...

edges.title: Metoprolol + Amlodipine: Low
edges.description: Amlodipine is dihydropyridine CCB with minimal cardiac effects (pure vasodilator). No significant interaction with beta blockers. Often used together for hypertension. Safe combination; monitor BP but...

edges.title: Carvedilol + Amlodipine: Low
edges.description: Safe combination commonly used in hypertension and heart failure. Amlodipine does not affect cardiac conduction. Monitor BP (can be additive), but generally well-tolerated. Preferred CCB when patient ...

edges.title: Digoxin + Furosemide: Medium
edges.description: Loop diuretic causes hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, which increase myocardial sensitivity to digoxin. Electrolyte abnormalities are major cause of digoxin toxicity (nausea, vomiting, arrhythmias, vis...

edges.title: Digoxin + Hydrochlorothiazide: Medium
edges.description: Thiazide-induced hypokalemia predisposes to digoxin toxicity. Chronic thiazide use often requires K+ supplementation or K+-sparing diuretic (spironolactone, amiloride). Check electrolytes every 3-6 mo...

edges.title: Digoxin + Verapamil: High
edges.description: Verapamil inhibits P-glycoprotein, reducing digoxin renal and biliary excretion. Increases digoxin levels 50-75%. Reduce digoxin dose by 50% when starting verapamil. Check digoxin level 5-7 days after...

edges.title: Digoxin + Diltiazem: Medium
edges.description: Similar mechanism to verapamil but weaker effect. Increases digoxin levels 20-30%. Consider empiric digoxin dose reduction of 25%. Monitor digoxin level and signs of toxicity (bradycardia, AV block, G...

edges.title: Digoxin + Spironolactone: Low
edges.description: Spironolactone may slightly increase digoxin levels and can interfere with digoxin immunoassay, causing falsely elevated lab results. True interaction minimal; more important is that spironolactone pr...

edges.title: Prednisone + Ibuprofen: Medium
edges.description: Both increase gastric ulcer risk independently. Combined use increases peptic ulcer incidence 4-5 fold. Mechanism: corticosteroids thin gastric mucosa, NSAIDs inhibit protective prostaglandins. High-r...

edges.title: Prednisone + Naproxen: Medium
edges.description: Similar to ibuprofen. Naproxen has longer half-life, providing sustained COX inhibition. GI bleeding risk higher with naproxen than shorter-acting NSAIDs. If combination necessary: use PPI (omeprazole...

edges.title: Prednisone + Aspirin: Medium
edges.description: Even low-dose aspirin (81mg) increases GI risk when combined with corticosteroids. Aspirin irreversibly inhibits platelet COX, affecting hemostasis for platelet lifespan (7-10 days). If both needed (e...

edges.title: Dexamethasone + Ibuprofen: Medium
edges.description: Dexamethasone is more potent than prednisone (6-7x). Higher GI toxicity potential. Avoid NSAID if possible; use acetaminophen for pain. If NSAID necessary, limit to 5-7 days maximum, use with PPI.

edges.title: Methylprednisolone + Naproxen: Medium
edges.description: Potent corticosteroid (4-5x prednisone) plus NSAID creates high GI risk. Commonly seen in rheumatologic disease flares where both may be used. Educate patient on warning signs: black stools (melena), ...

edges.title: Ibuprofen + Naproxen: Medium
edges.description: No additional anti-inflammatory benefit from combining NSAIDs. GI toxicity increases 2-3 fold. Renal toxicity also increased. Common error: patient taking OTC ibuprofen while on prescription naproxen,...

edges.title: Aspirin + Ibuprofen: Low
edges.description: Ibuprofen competes with aspirin for COX-1 binding site on platelets. If ibuprofen taken before aspirin, may block aspirin antiplatelet effect. Clinical relevance debated but concerning for CV protecti...

edges.title: Aspirin + Naproxen: Medium
edges.description: Both have antiplatelet effects but naproxen does not interfere with aspirin binding like ibuprofen. Still increases bleeding and GI risk without additional CV benefit. Generally avoid combination; if ...

edges.title: Aspirin + Meloxicam: Medium
edges.description: Meloxicam is COX-2 selective but still increases bleeding risk when combined with aspirin. No CV benefit beyond aspirin alone. Additive GI toxicity. Reserve meloxicam for inflammatory conditions; use ...

edges.title: Ibuprofen + Meloxicam: Medium
edges.description: Combining non-selective NSAID with COX-2 selective NSAID provides no benefit. Increases GI and renal adverse effects. Common in patients self-medicating with OTC ibuprofen while on prescription meloxi...

edges.title: Naproxen + Meloxicam: Medium
edges.description: Redundant NSAID therapy. Both have long half-lives (naproxen 12-17h, meloxicam 15-20h), increasing steady-state accumulation risk. Higher likelihood of renal dysfunction, edema, hypertension. Use sing...

edges.title: Diclofenac + Ibuprofen: Medium
edges.description: No rationale for combination. Diclofenac has highest CV risk among NSAIDs; adding ibuprofen increases this further. Avoid combination. If NSAID needed, choose one based on indication and patient risk ...

edges.title: Ciprofloxacin + Calcium Carbonate: Medium
edges.description: Divalent/trivalent cations (Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Zn2+) chelate fluoroquinolones in GI tract, forming non-absorbable complexes. Reduces ciprofloxacin bioavailability 50-90%, causing treatment failur...

edges.title: Levofloxacin + Calcium Carbonate: Medium
edges.description: Same chelation mechanism as ciprofloxacin. Levofloxacin bioavailability reduced significantly. Critical for serious infections (pneumonia, UTI). Dose separation essential: 2 hours before or 6 hours af...

edges.title: Ciprofloxacin + Iron Sulfate: Medium
edges.description: Iron forms particularly strong chelate with fluoroquinolones. Can reduce absorption >90%. Common in patients on iron for anemia. Must separate by at least 2 hours before or 6 hours after fluoroquinolo...

edges.title: Ciprofloxacin + Magnesium Oxide: Medium
edges.description: Magnesium supplement or magnesium-containing laxatives/antacids significantly impair fluoroquinolone absorption. Many OTC products contain magnesium; check labels. Separate dosing strictly. Alternativ...

edges.title: Doxycycline + Calcium Carbonate: Medium
edges.description: Tetracyclines form stable chelate complexes with polyvalent cations. Reduces doxycycline absorption by 50-90%. Classic teaching: "don't take tetracyclines with milk/dairy" (high calcium). Separate dos...

edges.title: Doxycycline + Iron Sulfate: Medium
edges.description: Iron-tetracycline chelation reduces absorption of both drugs. Problematic for patients needing long-term doxycycline (acne, rosacea) who are also iron deficient. Strict dose separation required. Monit...

edges.title: Levofloxacin + Iron Sulfate: Medium
edges.description: Similar to ciprofloxacin + iron. Separation critical. For elderly patients on multiple supplements, create dosing schedule: e.g., levofloxacin 8 AM, calcium + vitamin D noon, iron 6 PM. Ensure underst...

edges.title: Azithromycin + Calcium Carbonate: Low
edges.description: Macrolides not significantly affected by cation chelation. No dose separation needed. Safe to take azithromycin with or without food, supplements. Good alternative when patient has complex supplement ...

edges.title: Levothyroxine + Calcium Carbonate: Medium
edges.description: Calcium binds levothyroxine in GI tract, reducing absorption by 20-40%. Can lead to hypothyroid symptoms or need for dose increase. Take levothyroxine on empty stomach (30-60 min before breakfast), ca...

edges.title: Levothyroxine + Iron Sulfate: Medium
edges.description: Iron forms complex with levothyroxine, reducing absorption by 40-50%. Especially problematic in pregnancy (high iron needs + increased levothyroxine requirements). Strict separation: levothyroxine upo...

edges.title: Levothyroxine + Omeprazole: Low
edges.description: PPIs increase gastric pH, potentially reducing levothyroxine dissolution and absorption. Clinical significance controversial; some studies show effect, others don't. Monitor TSH 6-12 weeks after PPI i...

edges.title: Levothyroxine + Esomeprazole: Low
edges.description: Similar to omeprazole. Some patients may experience reduced levothyroxine absorption. More likely in elderly or those with atrophic gastritis. If TSH becomes elevated after starting PPI, consider: dos...

edges.title: Levothyroxine + Magnesium Oxide: Low
edges.description: Limited interaction data. Theoretically may reduce absorption via chelation. Separate doses as precaution (4 hours). More important for patients on high magnesium doses or with borderline hypothyroidi...

edges.title: Tamsulosin + Sildenafil: Medium
edges.description: Both cause vasodilation via different mechanisms. Tamsulosin: alpha-1 blockade in prostate and vasculature. Sildenafil: PDE5 inhibition increasing cGMP. Combined effect can cause symptomatic orthostat...

edges.title: Tamsulosin + Tadalafil: Medium
edges.description: Similar to sildenafil interaction. Tadalafil has longer half-life (17.5 hours), creating more sustained interaction. For once-daily tadalafil (BPH indication), use lower dose (2.5-5mg). For as-needed ...

edges.title: Sertraline + Ibuprofen: Medium
edges.description: SSRIs inhibit serotonin reuptake into platelets, impairing platelet aggregation. NSAIDs inhibit COX-1, reducing protective gastric prostaglandins. Synergistic effect increases upper GI bleeding risk 6...

edges.title: Sertraline + Aspirin: Medium
edges.description: Even low-dose aspirin (81mg) plus SSRI increases bleeding. Often both needed (depression + CV protection). Strongly recommend PPI. Monitor for: melena, hematemesis, anemia symptoms. Rare but serious: ...

edges.title: Escitalopram + Ibuprofen: Medium
edges.description: Escitalopram is potent SSRI with strong platelet serotonin depletion effect. Combined with NSAID, clinically significant bleeding risk. Alternatives: SNRI (if pain is indication - dual benefit), or ac...

edges.title: Fluoxetine + Naproxen: Medium
edges.description: Fluoxetine has long half-life; prolonged bleeding risk. Naproxen also has long half-life (12-17 hours). Both drugs present in system for extended periods, increasing sustained bleeding risk. Careful p...

edges.title: Citalopram + Aspirin: Medium
edges.description: Common combination in older adults (depression + CV disease). PPI prophylaxis recommended, especially if additional risk factors. Some evidence suggests proton pump inhibitor reduces upper GI bleeding...

edges.title: Paroxetine + Ibuprofen: Medium
edges.description: Paroxetine has strong serotonergic effect and anticholinergic properties. Combined with NSAID, increases GI bleeding risk. Also consider: paroxetine anticholinergic effects may slow GI motility, poten...

edges.title: Duloxetine + Ibuprofen: Medium
edges.description: SNRI (not pure SSRI) but still affects platelet serotonin. Duloxetine often used for chronic pain; NSAID may be redundant. Optimize duloxetine dose (60-120mg/day) before adding NSAID. If both needed, ...

edges.title: Albuterol + Metoprolol: Medium
edges.description: Beta-2 agonist antagonized by beta blocker. Metoprolol is "cardioselective" (beta-1 preferential) but not completely selective, especially at higher doses (>100mg/day). Can reduce albuterol bronchodil...

edges.title: Albuterol + Atenolol: Medium
edges.description: Similar to metoprolol. Atenolol is beta-1 selective. Generally safe in COPD but use cautiously in asthma. If patient develops increased dyspnea or wheezing after beta blocker initiation, consider: dos...

edges.title: Albuterol + Carvedilol: High
edges.description: Carvedilol is NON-selective beta blocker (blocks both beta-1 and beta-2). Significantly antagonizes albuterol effect and can cause severe bronchospasm in reactive airway disease. Generally CONTRAINDIC...

edges.title: Albuterol + Propranolol: High
edges.description: Propranolol is non-selective beta blocker. Absolutely contraindicated in asthma and generally avoided in COPD. Blocks beta-2 receptors in bronchi, causing bronchospasm. If patient on propranolol devel...

edges.title: Atorvastatin + Azithromycin: Low
edges.description: Minimal interaction. Azithromycin is weak CYP3A4 inhibitor; atorvastatin is CYP3A4 substrate. Short azithromycin course (3-5 days) unlikely to significantly increase statin levels. Monitor for muscle ...

edges.title: Simvastatin + Azithromycin: Medium

edges.title: Atorvastatin + Amlodipine: Low
